py05.summary: Ön işleme ve pipeline. Pipeline veri sızıntısını önler — scaler sadece train'e fit edilir.

sections.heading: Syntax
sections.text:
scaler.fit_transform(X_train)
scaler.transform(X_test)
make_pipeline(step1, step2, model)

sections.heading: Açıklama
sections.text: Ön işleme ve pipeline. Pipeline veri sızıntısını önler — scaler sadece train'e fit edilir.

sections.heading: Örnek
sections.text:
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler
from sklearn.pipeline import make_pipeline
from sklearn.feature_selection import SelectKBest, f_classif
from sklearn.svm import SVC

# Manuel scaling (dikkat: test'e sadece transform!)
scaler = StandardScaler()
X_train_s = scaler.fit_transform(X_train)  # fit + transform
X_test_s  = scaler.transform(X_test)       # sadece transform!

# Pipeline (önerilen — sızıntısız)
pipe = make_pipeline(
    StandardScaler(),
    SelectKBest(f_classif, k=5),
    SVC(kernel='rbf')
)
pipe.fit(X_train, y_train)   # tüm adımları eğit
pipe.predict(X_test)          # tüm adımları uygula
pipe.score(X_test, y_test)

sections.heading: Notlar
sections.text: fit_transform() = fit() + transform() birleşik. Test setinde sadece transform() kullan — aksi veri sızıntısı olur. cross_val_score() pipeline ile birlikte kullan.

py06.title: PyTorch Tensor Temelleri
py06.summary: PyTorch'un temel veri tipi Tensor. NumPy array'e benzer ama GPU'da çalışabilir ve autograd destekler.

sections.heading: Syntax
sections.text:
import torch
torch.tensor()  torch.zeros()  torch.randn()

sections.heading: Açıklama
sections.text: PyTorch'un temel veri tipi Tensor. NumPy array'e benzer ama GPU'da çalışabilir ve autograd destekler.

sections.heading: Örnek
sections.text:
import torch

torch.tensor([1.0, 2.0, 3.0])        # listeden
torch.zeros(3, 4)                     # sıfır
torch.ones(2, 3)                      # birler
torch.randn(3, 3)                     # normal dağılım
torch.arange(0, 10, 2)               # [0,2,4,6,8]
torch.linspace(0, 1, 5)

# NumPy ↔ Tensor
x_np = tensor.numpy()                 # tensor → numpy
tensor = torch.from_numpy(arr)        # numpy → tensor

# GPU'ya taşı
device = 'cuda' if torch.cuda.is_available() else 'cpu'
tensor = tensor.to(device)

# Shape ve info
tensor.shape    # torch.Size([3,4])
tensor.dtype    # torch.float32
tensor.device   # device(type='cpu')

sections.heading: Notlar
sections.text: Tensor'lar GPU'ya taşınınca tüm tensorların aynı device'da olması gerekir. CPU tensor → numpy direkt; GPU tensor → .cpu().numpy().

py06.title: PyTorch Model Tanımlama
py06.summary: PyTorch modelleri nn.Module'dan türetilir. __init__'te katmanlar tanımlanır, forward()'da hesaplama yazılır.

sections.heading: Syntax
sections.text:
class Net(nn.Module):
    def forward(self, x): ...

sections.heading: Açıklama
sections.text: PyTorch modelleri nn.Module'dan türetilir. __init__'te katmanlar tanımlanır, forward()'da hesaplama yazılır.

sections.heading: Örnek
sections.text:
import torch.nn as nn
import torch.nn.functional as F

class Net(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__()
        self.fc1 = nn.Linear(784, 128)  # in, out
        self.fc2 = nn.Linear(128, 64)
        self.fc3 = nn.Linear(64, 10)
        self.dropout = nn.Dropout(0.3)

    def forward(self, x):
        x = F.relu(self.fc1(x))
        x = self.dropout(x)
        x = F.relu(self.fc2(x))
        return self.fc3(x)   # son katmanda aktivasyon yok

model = Net().to(device)
print(model)               # katman özeti

# Parametre sayısı
sum(p.numel() for p in model.parameters())

sections.heading: Notlar
sections.text: nn.Sequential ile basit modeller daha kısa yazılır. Yaygın katmanlar: nn.Conv2d, nn.LSTM, nn.BatchNorm2d, nn.Embedding.

py06.title: PyTorch Training Loop
py06.summary: PyTorch training döngüsü: forward → loss → backward → optimize. Manuel ama tam kontrol sağlar.

sections.heading: Syntax
sections.text:
loss.backward()
optimizer.step()
optimizer.zero_grad()

sections.heading: Açıklama
sections.text: PyTorch training döngüsü: forward → loss → backward → optimize. Manuel ama tam kontrol sağlar.

sections.heading: Örnek
sections.text:
import torch.optim as optim

optimizer = optim.Adam(model.parameters(), lr=1e-3)
criterion = nn.CrossEntropyLoss()

for epoch in range(num_epochs):
    # --- Training ---
    model.train()               # dropout/bn aktif
    for X_batch, y_batch in train_loader:
        X_batch, y_batch = X_batch.to(device), y_batch.to(device)
        optimizer.zero_grad()   # gradyanları sıfırla
        out  = model(X_batch)   # forward pass
        loss = criterion(out, y_batch)
        loss.backward()         # backpropagation
        optimizer.step()        # ağırlıkları güncelle

    # --- Validation ---
    model.eval()                # dropout/bn kapalı
    with torch.no_grad():       # gradyan hesaplama
        val_loss = criterion(model(X_val), y_val)

sections.heading: Notlar
sections.text: zero_grad() unutulursa gradyanlar birikir → yanlış güncelleme. no_grad() validation'ı bellek ve hız açısından iyileştirir. DataLoader batch+shuffle yönetir.

py07.title: Big-O Cheat Sheet
py07.summary: Yaygın complexity'ler, DS işlem tablosu ve önemli notlar

sections.heading: Complexity Sınıfları (hızlıdan yavaşa)
items: O(1)      — Sabit: hash lookup, array index, stack push/pop
O(log n)  — Logaritmik: binary search, balanced BST ops
O(n)      — Lineer: single pass, linear scan
O(n log n) — Lineer-log: Merge sort, Heap sort, Timsort
O(n²)     — Karesel: bubble/insertion sort, nested loops
O(2^n)    — Üstel: recursive subsets, brute-force backtrack
O(n!)     — Faktöriyel: tüm permütasyonlar

sections.heading: Önemli Notlar
items: * = average case; worst case hash collision'da O(n) olabilir
Python sorted() ve list.sort() Timsort kullanır
C++ std::sort() Introsort (quicksort + heapsort + insertion sort)
Space complexity: recursive DFS O(h) — h = tree height
Amortize analiz: N push → toplam O(N), her push O(1) amortize

py07.title: Interview Patterns
py07.summary: Two Pointers, Sliding Window, BFS/DFS, DP ve diğer yaygın pattern'lar

sections.heading: Two Pointers
sections.text:
# Sorted array'de çift bul:
def two_sum_sorted(arr, target):
    l, r = 0, len(arr)-1
    while l < r:
        s = arr[l] + arr[r]
        if s == target: return [l, r]
        elif s < target: l += 1
        else: r -= 1
    return []

# Palindrome kontrolü:
def is_palindrome(s):
    l, r = 0, len(s)-1
    while l < r:
        if s[l] != s[r]: return False
        l += 1; r -= 1
    return True

sections.heading: Sliding Window
sections.text:
# Fixed window (size k):
def max_sum_subarray(arr, k):
    win = sum(arr[:k])
    best = win
    for i in range(k, len(arr)):
        win += arr[i] - arr[i-k]
        best = max(best, win)
    return best

# Variable window (at most k distinct):
def longest_k_distinct(s, k):
    count = {}; l = 0; best = 0
    for r, c in enumerate(s):
        count[c] = count.get(c,0)+1
        while len(count) > k:
            count[s[l]] -= 1
            if count[s[l]] == 0: del count[s[l]]
            l += 1
        best = max(best, r-l+1)
    return best

sections.heading: BFS — Shortest Path
DFS — Backtracking Template
Dynamic Programming — 1D / 2D

py07.title: Python vs C++ Syntax
py07.summary: Yaygın işlemlerin Python ve C++ karşılaştırması

py07.title: Hangi DS Ne Zaman?
py07.summary: Problem tipine göre doğru veri yapısı seçim rehberi

sections.heading: O(1) Lookup / Frequency
items: → Hash Map / Hash Set
Two Sum: complement = target-x in set
Anagram: Counter(s1) == Counter(s2)
Duplicate detection: set ile

sections.heading: Sıralı Veri / Range Query
items: → BST (set/map C++) veya sorted array + bisect
Floor/ceil: lower_bound / upper_bound
K-th smallest: BST veya QuickSelect
Range sum: Prefix sum array

sections.heading: En Küçük / En Büyük K Eleman
items: → Heap (Priority Queue)
K smallest: max-heap size k (Python: heapq.nsmallest)
K largest: min-heap size k
Median: iki heap (max-heap sol, min-heap sağ)

sections.heading: Graf Problemleri
items: → Adjacency list (defaultdict(list))
BFS: shortest path (unweighted), level order
DFS: cycle detection, topological sort, connected components
Weighted shortest: Dijkstra + min-heap
Dynamic connectivity: Union-Find

sections.heading: String Problemleri
items: → Trie: prefix search, autocomplete
→ Hash Map: character frequency
→ Two Pointers: palindrome, valid window
→ Sliding Window: longest substring with constraint
→ DP: edit distance, LCS, regex

sections.heading: Genel DP Tanıma
items: Optimal substructure + overlapping subproblems → DP
1D: Fibonacci, Climbing Stairs, Coin Change
2D: LCS, Edit Distance, Knapsack, Matrix paths
State machine: Stock buy/sell with cooldown
Interval: Merge intervals, meeting rooms

overviews.ch01:
Machine learning is the field where computers improve at tasks by learning from experience rather than being explicitly programmed. Before studying any specific algorithm, it is worth understanding what 'learning' actually means. Tom Mitchell's 1997 definition captures it precisely: a program learns from experience $E$ with respect to task $T$ and performance measure $P$ if its performance at $T$ improves with $E$. This abstraction covers everything in the course — $T$ could be classifying emails, $E$ the training dataset, and $P$ the classification accuracy.

Every ML problem reduces to finding a function $y = f(x; w)$ with parameters $w$ chosen to minimise a loss $\mathcal{L}$ over a training set. Classification outputs a discrete label $y \in \{C_1,\ldots,C_K\}$; regression outputs a real number $y \in \mathbb{R}$. The learning objective is $w^* = \arg\min_w \mathbb{E}_{(x,y)}[\mathcal{L}(y, f(x;w))]$, approximated in practice over the training set. The fundamental goal — easy to state, hard to achieve — is generalisation: $w^*$ must work on new, unseen data, not just memorise training examples.

The learning paradigm matters because it determines what data and infrastructure you need. Supervised learning, the course's focus, requires labeled $(x, t)$ pairs — the algorithm minimises a loss between predictions and targets. Unsupervised learning (GMMs, word embeddings) discovers structure in raw data without labels. Reinforcement learning uses environment rewards — conceptually distinct and out of scope here. Understanding which paradigm applies to a problem is the first design decision in any ML project.

Before any model, probability theory provides the language for reasoning under uncertainty. Two rules suffice for all probabilistic computation: the sum rule $p(X) = \sum_Y p(X,Y)$ (marginalising out unwanted variables) and the product rule $p(X,Y) = p(Y|X)p(X)$ (factorising joint distributions). Bayes theorem is simply the product rule applied twice and divided: $p(Y|X) = p(X|Y)p(Y)/p(X)$. It converts $p(\text{observation}|\text{hypothesis})$ — what the model generates — into $p(\text{hypothesis}|\text{observation})$ — what we need for inference. The prior $p(C_k)$, likelihood $p(x|C_k)$, and posterior $p(C_k|x)$ are introduced here and reappear in every chapter.

The chapter closes with the bias-variance trade-off — arguably the deepest single insight in all of ML. A polynomial of degree $N-1$ interpolates $N$ training points perfectly but oscillates catastrophically between them, failing on new data. This is overfitting: the model memorised the training noise. Underfitting is the opposite: a model too simple to capture the true pattern. Regularisation navigates between them by adding $\frac{\lambda}{2}\|w\|^2$ to the loss — penalising complexity. Large $\lambda$ forces simple models (high bias, low variance); small $\lambda$ allows complex models (low bias, high variance). The optimal $\lambda$ is found by cross-validation on a held-out validation set, never touching the test set. This discipline applies to every model in the course.
overviews.ch02:
Bayes Decision Theory asks: if we had perfect knowledge of the data distribution, what would the theoretically optimal classifier look like? The answer establishes the gold standard that every practical ML classifier tries to approximate.

The framework rests on three quantities that recur throughout the course. The prior $p(C_k)$ encodes base rates — how common is spam in all email? The likelihood $p(x|C_k)$ answers: given class $k$, how probable is observation $x$? A cluster of 'buy now!' words is more probable under spam. Bayes theorem converts these into the posterior $p(C_k|x) = p(x|C_k)p(C_k)/p(x)$ — the probability of each class given the specific $x$ we received. The optimal decision rule is simply $k^* = \arg\max_k p(C_k|x)$: pick the class with the highest posterior. This minimises the misclassification rate and is called the MAP (maximum a posteriori) decision rule.

A critical — and frequently violated — insight: classify by the posterior, not the likelihood. Ignoring prior probabilities leads to the base-rate neglect error. Consider a disease test with 99% sensitivity: if the disease affects only 0.1% of the population, a positive test gives only ~9% posterior probability of actually having the disease. The posterior automatically corrects for base rates; the likelihood does not. This is the mathematical reason why interpreting any test result requires knowing the prior prevalence.

Simply minimising misclassification rate is not always the right objective. In medical diagnosis, missing cancer (false negative) is far more costly than an unnecessary biopsy (false positive). A loss matrix $L_{kj}$ encodes the cost of predicting class $j$ when the truth is $k$. The optimal decision minimises expected risk $R(C_j|x) = \sum_k L_{kj}\,p(C_k|x)$ — shifting the decision boundary toward the more costly error. When uncertainty is too high even for the best classification, the reject option allows abstaining: a constant loss $\theta$ triggers 'refuse to classify' when $\max_k p(C_k|x) < 1-\theta$. This is essential in medical AI, self-driving systems, and safety-critical applications.

The chapter distinguishes three approaches to building classifiers, which recur as competing paradigms throughout the course. Generative models (LDA, QDA, Naive Bayes, GMMs) learn $p(x|C_k)$ and $p(C_k)$ separately, then apply Bayes theorem. They model the full data-generating process and can detect outliers, generate samples, and handle missing features — but waste capacity modelling $p(x)$, which doesn't directly help classification. Discriminative models (logistic regression) learn $p(C_k|x)$ directly, skipping the marginal. Discriminant function models (SVMs, deep networks) learn a score without any probabilistic interpretation. With the correct generative model and limited data, generative models are more sample-efficient. With abundant data and no distributional assumptions, discriminative models dominate.

LDA and QDA close the chapter with concrete instantiations. When class-conditionals are Gaussian with equal covariance, the log-posterior ratio is linear in $x$: the Bayes-optimal boundary is a hyperplane $w^\top x + w_0 = 0$ with $w = \Sigma^{-1}(\mu_1-\mu_2)$ (LDA). With different covariances, the boundary is quadratic (QDA). LDA needs one shared covariance ($O(D^2)$ parameters); QDA needs one per class ($O(KD^2)$). Remarkably, LDA under Gaussian assumptions produces exactly the logistic regression model — the same functional form, estimated differently. This equivalence bridges chapters 2 and 4.

Lab bağlantısı — Lab Session 1 (Basics): Bu chapter'ın pratik karşılığı. Iris dataset üzerinde KNN ve SVM ile sınıflandırma, confusion matrix ve accuracy hesaplama. PCA boyut indirgeme (manuel NumPy implementasyonu + sklearn karşılaştırması). Bayes decision theory'nin temelindeki sınıf olasılıklarını hissedebileceğin en doğrudan uygulama.
overviews.ch03:
Generative classifiers need $p(x|C_k)$ estimated from training data. Chapter 3 asks how to do this rigorously when $x$ is a continuous $D$-dimensional vector. The answer: assume $p(x|C_k)$ belongs to a parametric family, then fit the parameters from data. The Gaussian distribution is the canonical choice throughout the course.

The Gaussian is justified on multiple grounds. The Central Limit Theorem guarantees that sums of many independent random effects converge to Gaussian — and many measurement processes can be modelled this way. More formally, the Gaussian is the maximum entropy distribution given fixed mean and variance: it makes the fewest assumptions beyond these two moments. For a $D$-dimensional input, $\mathcal{N}(x;\mu,\Sigma) = (2\pi)^{-D/2}|\Sigma|^{-1/2}\exp(-\frac{1}{2}(x-\mu)^\top\Sigma^{-1}(x-\mu))$ has mean $\mu$ and covariance $\Sigma$. The exponent is the Mahalanobis distance — a generalisation of Euclidean distance that accounts for correlations and different scales. Contours of equal probability density are ellipsoids.

The covariance structure is a critical bias-variance trade-off. A full $\Sigma$ has $D(D+1)/2$ parameters — with 100 features, that's 5050 numbers per class, requiring thousands of samples to estimate reliably. A diagonal $\Sigma$ (the Naive Bayes assumption: features are conditionally independent given the class) needs only $D$ parameters. An isotropic $\sigma^2 I$ needs just 1. Restricting covariance structure reduces expressiveness but dramatically improves estimation reliability with limited data. LDA uses a shared full covariance across classes; QDA uses a separate full covariance per class.

Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE) finds $\theta_{\text{MLE}} = \arg\max_\theta \sum_n \ln p(x_n|\theta)$. For the Gaussian, MLE gives the sample mean (unbiased) and sample variance with $1/N$ (biased — the corrected estimator uses $1/(N-1)$, Bessel's correction). The bias occurs because the estimated mean $\hat\mu$ minimises squared deviations from the data, sitting slightly closer to the observations than the true $\mu$, systematically underestimating variance. The bias is $\sigma^2/N$, negligible for large $N$ but significant for small datasets.

The Bayesian view treats parameters as random variables. A prior $p(\theta)$ encodes beliefs; Bayes theorem gives the posterior $p(\theta|\mathcal{X}) \propto p(\mathcal{X}|\theta)p(\theta)$. MAP (Maximum A-Posteriori) finds the mode of the posterior: $\theta_{\text{MAP}} = \arg\max[\ln p(\mathcal{X}|\theta) + \ln p(\theta)]$. With a Gaussian prior $\mathcal{N}(0,\sigma_0^2)$, MAP is algebraically identical to MLE plus L2 regularisation with $\lambda = 1/\sigma_0^2$. This is a beautiful bridge: a Bayesian prior and a regulariser are the same thing, viewed from different philosophies. A strong prior (small $\sigma_0^2$) = aggressive regularisation.

When a single Gaussian is too restrictive — data has multiple clusters or non-Gaussian structure — Gaussian Mixture Models extend it: $p(x) = \sum_{k=1}^K \pi_k\,\mathcal{N}(x;\mu_k,\Sigma_k)$. Direct MLE fails because the log-likelihood $\sum_n \ln\sum_k \pi_k \mathcal{N}(\cdot)$ involves a log of a sum with no closed form. The EM algorithm alternates between E-step (compute soft cluster assignments $r_{nk}$, the posterior probability that component $k$ generated $x_n$) and M-step (re-estimate all parameters as weighted averages using $r_{nk}$). Log-likelihood is guaranteed to increase at every iteration, converging to a local maximum. Multiple random initialisations and taking the best result is standard practice.

Lab bağlantısı — Lab Session 1 (Basics): GMM ve KMeans ile unsupervised clustering, Iris dataset üzerinde. KMeans'ın EM algoritmasının hard-assignment versiyonu olduğunu gözlemleyebilirsin. Aynı session'da z-score ve min-max normalizasyon, veri ön işleme pipeline'ı.
overviews.ch04:
Having established Bayes-optimal classifiers (chapter 2) and how to estimate class-conditional densities (chapter 3), this chapter asks: can we skip modelling $p(x|C_k)$ entirely and directly learn the decision boundary? Yes — this is the discriminative approach. It is computationally simpler, more robust to violated distributional assumptions, and forms the foundation of logistic regression and all neural network classifiers.

The simplest discriminative model is the linear discriminant function $y(x) = w^\top x + w_0$. The decision boundary $y(x)=0$ is a hyperplane perpendicular to $w$. The signed distance from any point $x$ to the boundary is $y(x)/\|w\|$, so the discriminant value directly measures classification confidence. For two classes, assign $C_1$ if $y(x) > 0$. For $K$ classes, maintain $K$ linear functions and assign the class with the highest score — no ambiguous regions, with convex polytope decision regions. This multi-class setup is exactly what softmax produces in neural networks, connecting directly to chapter 7.

The most naive approach to training discriminants is least-squares: minimise $\|\tilde{X}W - T\|^2$ where $T$ is a one-hot target matrix. The closed-form solution $W = \tilde{X}^\dagger T$ (pseudo-inverse) is computationally appealing but fundamentally wrong for classification. The squared loss penalises predictions that are 'too correct': a point clearly on the right side of the boundary with output $y=5$ (target $t=1$) incurs loss $(5-1)^2=16$, pulling the boundary toward it. Outlier clusters on the correct side can completely invert the boundary. This is a structural flaw, not a tuning issue.

Logistic regression fixes this by modelling the posterior directly: $p(C_1|x) = \sigma(w^\top x + w_0)$ where $\sigma(a) = 1/(1+e^{-a})$ is the sigmoid. Training uses the cross-entropy loss $E(w) = -\sum_n [t_n\ln y_n + (1-t_n)\ln(1-y_n)]$, which is convex in $w$, guaranteeing a unique global minimum reachable by gradient descent. The gradient is the clean formula $\sum_n(y_n - t_n)x_n$ — zero when predictions match targets, large where the model is wrong, never penalising confidence. There is no closed-form solution, but IRLS (Iteratively Reweighted Least Squares, equivalent to Newton's method) converges in few iterations. For $K$ classes, the sigmoid generalises to softmax: $p(C_k|x) = \exp(w_k^\top x) / \sum_j \exp(w_j^\top x)$ — outputs are positive and sum to 1. This is the standard output of every neural network multi-class classifier.

The chapter closes with a remarkable theoretical bridge: if class-conditional distributions are Gaussian with equal covariance — the LDA assumption from chapter 2 — then applying Bayes theorem gives exactly the logistic sigmoid of a linear function. LDA and logistic regression produce the same model, estimated differently. LDA fits each class density separately (generative, MLE on Gaussians): more sample-efficient when Gaussian assumptions hold. Logistic regression maximises conditional likelihood $p(t|x,w)$ directly (discriminative): more robust when assumptions are violated, asymptotically better with large data. This equivalence shows that the generative/discriminative distinction is not about the model form but about the estimation principle.
overviews.ch05:
Linear classifiers (chapter 4) work well when data is linearly separable, but which of infinitely many correct hyperplanes should we choose? SVMs answer with a principled criterion grounded in statistical learning theory: choose the maximum-margin hyperplane — the one with the largest gap between the two classes. VC theory shows that the generalisation error bound is $O(1/\text{margin})$, so the widest-margin hyperplane has the strongest theoretical guarantee.

The hard-margin SVM formulates this as a constrained quadratic programme. After rescaling $w$ so that the nearest points (support vectors) satisfy $|w^\top x + b| = 1$, the margin width is $2/\|w\|$. Maximising margin means minimising $\|w\|^2$: $\min_{w,b}\frac{1}{2}\|w\|^2$ subject to $t_n(w^\top x_n + b) \geq 1$ for all $n$. This is convex with a unique global minimum, unlike the non-convex landscapes of neural networks. The constraints are linear in $w$, making this a standard convex QP efficiently solvable by modern optimisers.

The Lagrangian dual is where the real power lies. Introducing multipliers $a_n \geq 0$ per constraint and optimising, the KKT stationarity conditions give $w = \sum_n a_n t_n x_n$ — the weight vector is a linear combination of training points. The KKT complementarity condition $a_n(t_n y(x_n)-1) = 0$ implies either $a_n = 0$ (point is correctly outside the margin, plays no role) or $t_n y(x_n)=1$ (point sits exactly on the margin boundary — a support vector). Typically only 5–30% of training points are support vectors; the rest are irrelevant to $w$. Crucially, the dual objective depends on data only through inner products $x_n^\top x_m$ — this is exactly what the kernel trick exploits.

Real data is rarely linearly separable. Soft-margin SVMs introduce slack variables $\xi_n \geq 0$ allowing violations: $t_n(w^\top x_n+b) \geq 1-\xi_n$. The objective becomes $\min\frac{1}{2}\|w\|^2 + C\sum_n\xi_n$ — a trade-off between margin width and constraint violations controlled by $C$. Interpreting $\xi_n$: $\xi_n=0$ means correctly classified outside margin; $\xi_n \in (0,1)$ means inside margin; $\xi_n > 1$ means misclassified. Large $C$ strongly penalises violations (narrow margin, low training error, risk of overfitting). Small $C$ accepts many violations (wide margin, more regularised). In the dual, the change is simply $0 \leq a_n \leq C$ — still convex, efficient to solve.

The kernel trick enables non-linear classification without changing the dual structure. Replace every inner product $x_n^\top x_m$ with $k(x_n, x_m) = \phi(x_n)^\top\phi(x_m)$ — where $\phi$ maps to a (potentially infinite-dimensional) feature space. The Gaussian/RBF kernel $k(x,y) = \exp(-\|x-y\|^2/2\sigma^2)$ corresponds to an infinite-dimensional feature space, computed in $O(D)$ time. Mercer's theorem ensures any positive semi-definite function is a valid kernel. With the kernel trick, SVMs find a linear boundary in the feature space — which corresponds to an arbitrary non-linear boundary in the original space. SVMs dominated the 2000s before deep learning; they remain competitive on tabular and structured data with moderate $N$, and the kernel idea influenced Gaussian Processes and kernel methods broadly.

Lab bağlantısı — Lab Session 1 (Basics): Iris dataset üzerinde SVM sınıflandırması. Session 1'in KNN ve GMM ile aynı notebook'ta olduğu için tek bir oturumda üç farklı sınıflandırıcıyı karşılaştırma imkânı var.
overviews.ch06:
All models so far are single learners. Ensemble methods combine multiple models to produce a stronger classifier — a committee that outperforms any individual member. This chapter covers two distinct ensemble strategies: bagging (parallel, variance reduction) and boosting (sequential, bias+variance reduction), with AdaBoost as the canonical boosting algorithm.

Bagging (Bootstrap Aggregating) trains $M$ models on $M$ bootstrap datasets — each of size $N$, drawn with replacement from the original, containing approximately 63% unique points. Ensemble predictions are averaged. If individual errors are uncorrelated with variance $\sigma^2$, averaging reduces ensemble variance to $\sigma^2/M$. In practice, models trained on overlapping data are correlated ($\rho > 0$), so variance converges to $\rho\sigma^2$ as $M\to\infty$ — the correlation bottleneck. Bagging is most effective for complex, unstable models (deep decision trees) where variance dominates over bias. It cannot fix underfitting: a biased model stays biased in an ensemble. Random Forests extend bagging by also randomising the feature subset at each split, reducing the correlation between trees and achieving further variance reduction.

AdaBoost (Freund & Schapire, 1996) takes a fundamentally different approach: sequential ensemble construction where each new model focuses on the previous models' mistakes. Starting with uniform weights $w_n^{(1)} = 1/N$, AdaBoost iteratively: (1) trains a weak learner $h_m$ on the weighted training set; (2) computes its weighted error $\varepsilon_m$; (3) assigns it ensemble weight $\alpha_m = \frac{1}{2}\ln\frac{1-\varepsilon_m}{\varepsilon_m}$; (4) upweights misclassified examples by $e^{\alpha_m}$, downweights correctly classified ones. Final prediction: $H(x) = \text{sign}(\sum_m \alpha_m h_m(x))$. A weak learner only needs to be slightly better than chance ($\varepsilon_m < 0.5$); decision stumps (single-threshold classifiers) suffice. Learners that perform near random get $\alpha_m \approx 0$; near-perfect learners get $\alpha_m \to \infty$.

The theoretical justification is elegant and surprising: AdaBoost can be derived as coordinate-wise gradient descent on the exponential loss $E = \sum_n \exp(-t_n f(x_n))$. The sample weight $w_n^{(m)} \propto \exp(-t_n f_{m-1}(x_n))$ is exactly the negative gradient of $E$ with respect to the current ensemble prediction. This reinterpretation spawned the gradient boosting family: replace the exponential loss with any differentiable loss (logistic, squared, absolute), fit each new model to the negative gradient (pseudo-residuals), and you get a general-purpose boosting framework. Gradient boosted trees (XGBoost, LightGBM, CatBoost) — which implement exactly this idea — dominate tabular data competitions today.

The exponential loss grows exponentially for confident mispredictions, making AdaBoost extremely sensitive to label noise: a single mislabeled point can receive exponentially large weight, corrupting the ensemble. More robust losses (logistic, Huber) give noise-resistant gradient boosting variants. Comparing bagging and boosting: bagging is parallel and reduces variance; boosting is sequential and reduces both bias and variance (at the cost of noise sensitivity). Bagging works with complex base models; boosting works with simple ones. Both substantially outperform any single model on most benchmarks.
overviews.ch07:
All models so far — from logistic regression to SVMs — learn a linear function in either the original or a kernel-induced feature space. The features themselves are hand-engineered or fixed. Neural networks take a different approach: learn the feature representations jointly with the classifier, end-to-end from raw data. This is the key capability that makes deep learning transformative.

The history of neural networks is a story of hype, setbacks, and eventual triumph. The perceptron (Rosenblatt, 1957) could learn any linearly separable function. Minsky and Papert (1969) proved it couldn't learn XOR (not linearly separable) — no single hyperplane can separate $\{(0,0)\to0,(1,1)\to0\}$ from $\{(0,1)\to1,(1,0)\to1\}$. The fix requires one hidden layer: hidden units learn intermediate features that ARE linearly separable, effectively computing a non-linear feature map. Backpropagation (Rumelhart et al., 1986) made multi-layer training feasible. AlexNet (2012) demonstrated that deep CNNs with GPUs and large datasets could dramatically outperform classical feature engineering on ImageNet, starting the deep learning era.

An MLP stacks layers of linear transformations followed by non-linear activations: $a^{(l)} = g(W^{(l)}a^{(l-1)} + b^{(l)})$ where $g$ is the elementwise activation. Without $g$, any composition of linear functions collapses to a single linear function — depth would be meaningless. The activation function is the essential ingredient. ReLU $\max(0,z)$ is the standard choice: gradient is 0 or 1 (no attenuation through depth), no saturation for positive inputs, sparse activations. The Universal Approximation Theorem guarantees that even a single hidden layer with enough neurons can approximate any continuous function — but 'enough' can be exponentially many. Depth achieves the same expressive power with polynomially many neurons: functions requiring $2^D$ neurons with one layer may need only $O(D^2)$ neurons with multiple layers.

Backpropagation computes gradients of the loss with respect to all parameters in a single backward pass. Define error signals $\delta^{(l)} = \partial\mathcal{L}/\partial z^{(l)}$. At the output layer: $\delta^{(L)} = (\partial\mathcal{L}/\partial a^{(L)}) \odot g'(z^{(L)})$. Propagate backwards: $\delta^{(l)} = (W^{(l+1)})^\top \delta^{(l+1)} \odot g'(z^{(l)})$. Gradients for weights: $\partial\mathcal{L}/\partial W^{(l)} = \delta^{(l)}(a^{(l-1)})^\top$. This computes ALL gradients in $O(1)$ passes — not one pass per parameter — making training feasible for millions of parameters. In deep networks, the product of weight matrices and activation derivatives can cause gradients to vanish (if the product $< 1$ at each layer) or explode (if $> 1$). ReLU mitigates vanishing; residual connections (chapter 9) and batch normalisation (chapter 8) further address it.

The output activation and loss function are always paired: sigmoid + binary cross-entropy for binary classification, softmax + categorical cross-entropy for multi-class, linear + MSE for regression. These pairings are not arbitrary — the activation derivative exactly cancels with the loss derivative through the chain rule, giving the clean output delta $\delta = \hat{y} - y$ (prediction error). This cancellation prevents gradient saturation at the output layer and is why these specific pairings are always used together.

Lab bağlantısı — Lab Session 3 (Neural Network & Backprop): MLP'yi tamamen sıfırdan NumPy ile implement etme — hiç PyTorch/Keras yok. Linear layer forward ve backward pass manuel yazılıyor, MSE loss, sigmoid aktivasyonu ve türevi, SGD güncelleme adımları. Sonra SoftMax + Cross Entropy eklenerek MNIST üzerinde eğitim. Backprop'un her adımını elle görmek için en doğrudan lab.
overviews.ch08:
Training a neural network is a non-convex optimisation problem over millions of parameters. Unlike the convex objectives of logistic regression and SVMs, there is no guarantee of reaching the global minimum. Yet deep networks achieve remarkable performance. This chapter explains the practical optimisation techniques that make this possible.

Gradient descent updates $w \leftarrow w - \eta\nabla_w E(w)$. The learning rate $\eta$ is the most critical hyperparameter: too large causes divergence; too small causes impractically slow convergence. Batch GD uses all $N$ training points for an exact gradient but is prohibitively expensive for large datasets. SGD uses one random point per update — noisy, but fast and scalable. Mini-batch SGD (batch size $B \approx 32$–$512$) strikes the practical balance: fast iterations, GPU-parallelisable computation, and moderate noise. Importantly, the noise in SGD gradients is not just a necessary evil — it acts as implicit regularisation, steering the optimiser toward flat minima (wide loss basins) that generalise better than the sharp minima found by exact gradient descent.

Plain SGD struggles with ill-conditioned landscapes: it oscillates across steep dimensions while barely moving along flat ones. Momentum accumulates gradient history $v \leftarrow \beta v + (1-\beta)\nabla E$, then updates $w \leftarrow w - \eta v$ ($\beta \approx 0.9$). Momentum dampens oscillations along steep dimensions (alternating gradients cancel) and accelerates along flat ones (consistent gradients accumulate). Adam (Adaptive Moment Estimation) goes further: it maintains a running mean $m_t$ and running variance $v_t$ of gradients, dividing the update by $\sqrt{\hat{v}_t}$. This gives each parameter its own effective learning rate — large for sparse/small-gradient parameters (word embeddings for rare words), small for large-gradient parameters (to prevent oscillation). After bias correction for the initialisation at zero, Adam converges reliably with default hyperparameters ($\beta_1=0.9$, $\beta_2=0.999$, $\eta=10^{-3}$) across nearly all deep learning architectures. It is the standard optimiser.

Batch Normalisation (Ioffe & Szegedy, 2015) addresses internal covariate shift: as earlier layers update, the distribution of their outputs shifts, forcing later layers to constantly adapt. BN normalises each layer's activations to zero mean and unit variance within each mini-batch, then rescales with learnable parameters $\gamma$ and $\beta$: $y_i = \gamma\hat{x}_i + \beta$ where $\hat{x}_i = (x_i - \mu_{\mathcal{B}})/\sqrt{\sigma^2_{\mathcal{B}}+\varepsilon}$. This stabilises the input distribution to each layer, enabling higher learning rates, better gradient flow (activations stay away from sigmoid saturation), and mild regularisation from batch statistics noise. At test time, running averages of training statistics replace per-batch statistics — the reason models must be switched between train and eval modes.

Weight initialisation determines whether gradients vanish or explode from the very first step. Glorot initialisation $\text{Var}(W) = 2/(n_{\text{in}}+n_{\text{out}})$ keeps signal variance approximately constant through the network for symmetric activations. He initialisation doubles this for ReLU, which zeros half its inputs. Dropout randomly deactivates neurons with probability $p$ during training, forcing redundancy: no single neuron can be exclusively relied upon. At test time, all neurons are active with weights scaled by $(1-p)$. Interpreted as an ensemble of $2^N$ subnetworks, dropout is one of the most effective regularisers for fully-connected layers. Batch normalisation often reduces the need for dropout in convolutional networks.

Lab bağlantısı — Lab Session 2 (Loss & Metric): PyTorch ve TorchMetrics ile loss fonksiyonları ve metrikler. Regresyon için MSE/MAE, sınıflandırma için CE/BCE, segmentasyon için Dice coefficient, binary classification için ROC + AUROC. Görüntü kalitesi için SSIM. Hangi loss'un hangi durumda kullanıldığını ve neden seçildiğini karşılaştırmalı görme imkânı.
overviews.ch09:
Applying fully-connected networks to images is both computationally infeasible and statistically counterproductive. A single FC layer for a $224\times224\times3$ image has 150 million parameters — just for one layer. More fundamentally, FC layers ignore the structure of images: moving a cat one pixel to the right changes all 150k inputs, requiring the network to re-learn 'cat recognition' for every spatial position. CNNs solve this by encoding three domain-specific properties directly into the architecture.

Natural images exhibit locality (nearby pixels are correlated; distant ones are often independent), translation equivariance (a cat looks the same whether top-left or bottom-right), and hierarchical composition (Hubel & Wiesel, 1959: simple cells detect oriented edges; complex cells detect the same edge anywhere; higher areas detect shapes and objects). CNNs implement these through convolutional layers (local filters with weight sharing: the same $3\times3$ filter slides across the entire image), pooling (local translation invariance and spatial downsampling), and layer stacking (hierarchical feature composition). A $3\times3$ filter with $C_{\text{in}}$ input channels has $9C_{\text{in}}$ parameters, regardless of image size. With 64 filters: $9\times64\times C_{\text{in}}$ parameters vs $64\times W\times H\times C_{\text{in}}$ for FC — a $W\times H/9$ reduction, typically $5000\times$ fewer parameters.

Stacking convolutional layers grows the receptive field without growing the parameter count: $L$ layers of $3\times3$ filters cover a $(2L+1)\times(2L+1)$ input region. Early layers detect edges and textures; middle layers detect shapes and parts; deep layers detect semantic categories. This hierarchy mirrors the brain's visual cortex and is what makes CNNs work: relevant features are progressively abstracted. Pooling (typically $2\times2$ max pooling with stride 2) downsamples the feature map by $4\times$ while providing local translation invariance. Modern architectures increasingly replace pooling with strided convolutions (learnable downsampling).

CNN architectures evolved rapidly by addressing successive bottlenecks. AlexNet (2012): ReLU activations (no vanishing gradients), GPU training, dropout regularisation — first to beat classical features at scale. VGGNet (2014): systematic $3\times3$ convolutions, same receptive field as larger kernels with fewer parameters and more non-linearity. GoogLeNet (2014): Inception modules with parallel branches of different kernel sizes — the network learns which scale to use. ResNet (2015): residual connections $\text{output} = F(x) + x$ solved the degradation problem — deeper networks were getting worse, not due to overfitting but due to optimisation difficulty. Skip connections provide gradient highways and make learning the identity easy: if $F(x)\approx0$, the output is simply $x$. ResNets enabled 50, 100, 152-layer networks and human-level ImageNet accuracy.

Object detection extends classification to localisation: predict both 'what' and 'where'. The R-CNN family progressively optimised this: R-CNN (apply CNN to 2000 proposals, 47s/image) → Fast R-CNN (extract features once, RoI pooling per region, 2s/image) → Faster R-CNN (learn proposals with RPN, end-to-end, 0.2s/image). YOLO (2016) reformulated detection as a single regression problem: a $S\times S$ grid, each cell predicting $B$ bounding boxes and class probabilities in one forward pass — 45 FPS real-time detection. The two-stage vs single-stage accuracy-speed trade-off has been progressively closing; DETR (2020) replaced hand-engineered anchors and NMS with Transformers and bipartite matching, unifying detection with the same architecture used for NLP.

Lab bağlantısı — Lab Session 4 + Session 5: Session 4'te Pascal VOC 2012 üzerinde VGG-16 multi-label classification (VGG block sıfırdan) ve BasicResnetBlock ile skip connection implement etme, Top-1/Top-5 accuracy. Session 5'te aynı dataset üzerinde semantic segmentation — VGG'nin FC katmanlarını Conv'a dönüştürerek FCN yapma, transposed convolution ile upsampling, skip connection ile multi-scale feature fusion, mIoU ve Dice score değerlendirme. Bonus: tam U-Net mimarisi.
overviews.ch10:
All models so far treat inputs as real-valued vectors with inherent geometry. Text is different: words are discrete symbolic tokens. One-hot encodings give no notion of similarity — 'dog' and 'cat' are as distant as 'dog' and 'democracy'. Word embeddings solve this by mapping each word to a continuous vector where geometric proximity reflects semantic similarity. This chapter explains how these representations are learned and what they capture.

The theoretical foundation is the distributional hypothesis (Firth, 1957): 'you shall know a word by the company it keeps.' Words appearing in similar contexts have similar meanings — 'dog' and 'cat' both appear near 'pet', 'feed', 'vet'. This enables entirely unsupervised learning: no labels, just raw text. Classical n-gram models estimated $p(w_t|w_{t-1},\ldots,w_{t-n+1})$ by counting co-occurrences, but face a fundamental sparsity problem: with a vocabulary of 50,000 words, a 5-gram model has $50000^4 \approx 10^{18}$ possible contexts, most never observed. 'The cat sat on the mat' and 'The dog lay on the floor' share zero 3-grams despite similar meaning — n-grams cannot generalise across similar words.

Bengio's neural language model (2003) solved this by representing words as learned real-valued vectors. Given these embeddings, similar words have similar contexts, and the model can generalise: even if 'The dog lay' never appeared in training, the similarity of 'dog'/'cat' and 'lay'/'sat' in embedding space allows probability estimation. Word2Vec (Mikolov et al., 2013) simplified this to a dedicated embedding-learning objective. Skip-gram: given centre word $w_t$, predict context words in window $[-k,k]$ by maximising $\sum_t\sum_{j}\log p(w_{t+j}|w_t)$ where $p(w_O|w_I) = \exp(u_{w_O}^\top v_{w_I})/\sum_w\exp(u_w^\top v_{w_I})$. CBOW: predict centre from context (average of context embeddings). Computing the softmax denominator over the full vocabulary is expensive; negative sampling approximates it by distinguishing real pairs from $K\approx5$–$20$ randomly sampled 'noise' pairs, reducing per-step cost from $O(|V|)$ to $O(K)$.

Well-trained embeddings exhibit remarkable algebraic structure: $v(\text{king}) - v(\text{man}) + v(\text{woman}) \approx v(\text{queen})$. The 'royalty' direction in embedding space ($v(\text{king}) - v(\text{man})$) is approximately parallel to the 'female royalty' direction — emerging purely from predicting co-occurrences, with no explicit semantic supervision. Similarly, $v(\text{Paris}) - v(\text{France}) + v(\text{Germany}) \approx v(\text{Berlin})$ (capital-country offset), and past-tense offsets are consistent across verbs. GloVe (Pennington, 2014) achieves similar results by directly factorising the global log co-occurrence matrix: $w_i^\top\tilde{w}_j + b_i + \tilde{b}_j = \log X_{ij}$. GloVe has faster convergence by encoding global statistics directly; Word2Vec scales better with corpus size.

These are static embeddings: one vector per word regardless of context. 'Bank' has the same embedding in 'river bank' and 'bank loan'. Contextual embeddings (ELMo, BERT, GPT — chapter 12) assign different vectors depending on context, capturing polysemy and long-range dependencies. Static embeddings remain useful as efficient baselines, in low-resource settings, and as the conceptual stepping stone to understanding Transformer-based models.
overviews.ch11:
Many real-world problems involve ordered sequences — text, audio, time series, genomic data. Unlike fixed-size vectors, sequences have variable length and position-dependent structure. RNNs address this by processing sequences step by step, maintaining a hidden state that accumulates context. Weight sharing across time makes them parameter-efficient and allows generalisation across positions.

The core RNN update $h_t = \sigma(W_{xh}x_t + W_{hh}h_{t-1} + b_h)$ combines the current input with the previous hidden state using the same matrices at every step — analogous to how CNNs share filters across space. The hidden state $h_t \in \mathbb{R}^H$ is a learned compressed representation of all past inputs $x_1,\ldots,x_t$. Its capacity is a strict bottleneck: for short sequences (5–15 tokens) this works; for long sequences, early information is progressively overwritten by new inputs. This information bottleneck motivates LSTM, GRU, and ultimately Transformers. RNNs support diverse input/output configurations: many-to-one (sentiment analysis — read the whole review, output one label), one-to-many (image captioning — input one image, generate many words), and many-to-many (translation — both encoder and decoder are sequences).

Training uses Backpropagation Through Time (BPTT): unroll the RNN for $T$ steps and apply backpropagation. The gradient of a loss at step $T$ with respect to hidden state $h_k$ requires the product $\prod_{i=k+1}^T \frac{\partial h_i}{\partial h_{i-1}} = \prod_{i=k+1}^T W_{hh}^\top \cdot \text{diag}(\sigma'(z_i))$. Each factor involves $W_{hh}$ (magnification) and sigmoid derivative $\sigma' \leq 0.25$ (attenuation). If the product $< 1$ at each step, gradients vanish exponentially — for $T=50$ steps and $\|W_{hh}\|\cdot\sigma' = 0.5$: gradient $\approx 0.5^{50} \approx 10^{-15}$. The network effectively cannot learn what happened 20+ steps ago. If the product $> 1$, gradients explode to infinity; this is easier to fix with gradient clipping.

LSTMs (Hochreiter & Schmidhuber, 1997) introduce a cell state $C_t$ running through the sequence with minimal modification. Four gates control information: forget gate $f_t = \sigma(W_f[h_{t-1},x_t])$ decides what to erase from memory; input gate $i_t$ decides what new information to write; candidate $g_t = \tanh(\cdot)$ provides new content; output gate $o_t$ controls what memory to expose as $h_t$. Cell update: $C_t = f_t \odot C_{t-1} + i_t \odot g_t$. The gradient through the cell state is $\partial C_t/\partial C_{t-1} = f_t$ — when $f_t \approx 1$, gradients flow without attenuation: a gradient highway. LSTMs reliably remember across 100–1000 steps. GRUs (Cho et al., 2014) simplify LSTMs to two gates (update and reset), merging cell and hidden state, with fewer parameters and similar empirical performance.

For sequence-to-sequence problems (translation, summarisation), an encoder compresses the input into a fixed vector, which the decoder reads to generate the output. This information bottleneck fails for long sequences. Attention (Bahdanau, 2015) solves it: at each decoding step $i$, compute alignment scores $e_{ij} = f_{\text{att}}(s_{i-1}, h_j)$ between the decoder state and each encoder hidden state $h_j$, normalise to attention weights $\alpha_{ij}$, and form a dynamic context vector $c_i = \sum_j\alpha_{ij}h_j$. The decoder can now focus on the relevant part of the input at each step. Visualising $\alpha_{ij}$ reveals interpretable alignment: to generate the French word 'chat', the decoder attends to the English word 'cat'. This mechanism is the direct conceptual precursor to Transformers: chapter 12 removes the RNN entirely and uses self-attention as the primary modelling mechanism.
overviews.ch12:
Transformers (Vaswani et al., 2017) are the architecture that replaced RNNs for sequence modelling and subsequently conquered computer vision, protein structure prediction, and scientific computing. Their key innovation is replacing sequential state-passing (RNNs) with a mechanism that processes entire sequences in parallel: self-attention. Every position attends directly to every other position in a single step, giving $O(1)$ maximum dependency path length (vs $O(n)$ for RNNs) and full GPU parallelism during training.

Self-attention works by projecting the input $X \in \mathbb{R}^{n\times d}$ into three matrices via learned linear projections: queries $Q = XW_Q$, keys $K = XW_K$, values $V = XW_V$. The output is $\text{Attention}(Q,K,V) = \text{softmax}(QK^\top/\sqrt{d_k})V$. Think of this as a differentiable database lookup: each position $i$ uses its query $Q_i$ to measure compatibility with every key $K_j$ (via dot product), applies softmax to get a probability distribution over positions, then takes a weighted sum of values. The $\sqrt{d_k}$ scaling prevents dot products from growing too large (which would push softmax to near-one-hot distributions with vanishing gradients). Since every position can directly access every other, long-range dependencies are learned as easily as short-range ones — unlike RNNs where distant information must propagate through many state updates.

Self-attention is permutation-equivariant: it has no notion of which position came first. Without positional information, 'cat bites dog' and 'dog bites cat' would have identical representations — catastrophic for language. Sinusoidal positional encodings inject unique position fingerprints by adding $\text{PE}(\text{pos},2i) = \sin(\text{pos}/10000^{2i/d})$ to each token embedding. Different frequencies across dimensions create a unique code for each position, and $\text{PE}(\text{pos}+k)$ is a linear function of $\text{PE}(\text{pos})$, allowing the model to learn relative positions. Multi-head attention runs $h$ parallel attention computations: $\text{MultiHead} = \text{Concat}(\text{head}_1,\ldots,\text{head}_h)W^O$ where each head has independent projections. With $h=8$ heads, $d=512$, each head operates in a 64-dimensional subspace, learning different relationship types simultaneously (syntactic dependencies, semantic roles, local co-occurrence).

The full Transformer stacks $N$ encoder blocks, each with: multi-head self-attention (any position to any position) followed by a position-wise feed-forward network (two-layer MLP applied independently per position). Both sub-layers have residual connections and Layer Norm: $x \leftarrow \text{LayerNorm}(x + F(x))$. Residuals prevent vanishing gradients in depth; Layer Norm (across features, not batch) stabilises variable-length sequences. The decoder adds masked causal self-attention (position $i$ only attends to $j\leq i$) and cross-attention (queries from decoder, keys/values from encoder output), enabling encoder-decoder translation models.

Pre-training on massive unlabeled text produces representations that transfer to virtually any NLP task with minimal fine-tuning. BERT (encoder-only, 2018) pre-trains by predicting 15% masked tokens using bidirectional context — ideal for understanding tasks. GPT (decoder-only, 2018–) pre-trains by predicting the next token: $\mathcal{L}(\theta) = \sum_t \log p(x_t|x_1,\ldots,x_{t-1};\theta)$. This deceptively simple objective, applied to billions of tokens, forces the model to internalise syntax, semantics, factual knowledge, and reasoning — because all are needed to predict the next word accurately. The representations transfer to classification, QA, translation, and code generation with zero or few labeled examples. Scaling laws (Kaplan et al., 2020) show loss follows power laws in model size, data, and compute — motivating the investment in ever-larger models. GPT-3 (175B parameters) exhibits emergent few-shot capabilities not present in smaller models. Transformers now dominate not just NLP but vision (ViT), protein folding (AlphaFold2), audio (Whisper), and reinforcement learning.
overviews.ida01:
Image Data Analysis begins where perception begins: with the digital image itself. Before any algorithm can detect objects, segment cells, or reconstruct three-dimensional scenes, it must grapple with how the physical world is encoded as discrete numbers. A digital image is formally a function $I: \mathbb{Z}^2 \to \{0, \ldots, 2^k-1\}$ mapping pixel coordinates $(x,y)$ to intensity values. For an 8-bit image, $k=8$ and each pixel takes one of 256 possible values. The step from continuous light to discrete numbers involves two separate processes: spatial sampling (how finely is the image grid?) and intensity quantisation (how many bits per pixel?). The Nyquist–Shannon sampling theorem provides the theoretical lower bound — the sampling frequency must be at least twice the highest spatial frequency in the scene to avoid aliasing. Aliasing manifests as moiré patterns and jagged diagonal edges, and is the reason cameras apply an optical anti-aliasing filter before the sensor.

Colour introduces a second representation challenge. The RGB colour space encodes light as additive mixtures of red, green, and blue channels — physically natural for display hardware, but perceptually non-uniform (equal RGB differences are not equally visible) and inconvenient for analysis (changing illumination rotates RGB values in all three channels simultaneously). The HSV colour space factorises colour into hue (the colour), saturation (the purity), and value (the brightness), making illumination-robust colour segmentation straightforward: threshold on hue while ignoring value. The LAB colour space is the gold standard for perceptual uniformity: a Euclidean distance $\Delta E$ in LAB matches perceived colour difference. These three representations — RGB, HSV, LAB — are the foundational vocabulary used in every subsequent chapter when images must be analysed or compared.

The histogram $h(v) = \#\{(x,y) : I(x,y) = v\}$ is the simplest global image statistic: it counts how many pixels take each intensity value, discarding all spatial information. Despite its simplicity, the histogram is remarkably informative: a bimodal histogram reveals a foreground-background structure ripe for thresholding (a concept developed into Otsu's method in chapter ida05); a heavily skewed histogram signals poor contrast. Histogram equalisation corrects contrast by redistributing intensities so that the cumulative distribution function (CDF) becomes approximately uniform. The equalisation mapping is $T(v) = (L-1) \cdot \text{CDF}(v)$, where $L$ is the number of intensity levels. Applied globally, equalisation can over-amplify noise; CLAHE (Contrast Limited Adaptive Histogram Equalisation) applies it locally in tiles with a clip limit, producing far more useful results in medical imaging contexts encountered in later chapters.

Connectivity defines what it means for two pixels to be neighbours, and is the foundation for all region-based analysis. Under 4-connectivity, a pixel at $(x,y)$ has neighbours directly above, below, left, and right — four in total. Under 8-connectivity, the four diagonal neighbours are added, giving eight total. The choice matters: a thin diagonal line may be 8-connected but not 4-connected, and the boundary of a 4-connected region is 8-connected and vice versa — a topological duality. Connected-component labelling assigns a unique integer label to each maximal connected region using flood fill, enabling object counting, centroid computation, and region-of-interest extraction. This operation appears in every segmentation pipeline discussed in chapter ida05.

Morphological operations act on binary images via a structuring element $B$ — a small binary shape (disk, cross, square) that defines the neighbourhood of interaction. Dilation $A \oplus B = \{z : B_z \cap A \neq \emptyset\}$ grows regions outward; erosion $A \ominus B = \{z : B_z \subseteq A\}$ shrinks them. These two operations chain into opening ($A \circ B = (A \ominus B) \oplus B$, erosion then dilation) which removes small objects and smooths boundaries, and closing ($A \bullet B = (A \oplus B) \ominus B$, dilation then erosion) which fills small holes. Opening and closing are idempotent — applying them twice gives the same result as once — a useful algebraic property. These elementary operations remain in use throughout the course: morphological watersheds underpin classical segmentation (ida05), and the structuring element foreshadows the convolution kernel introduced systematically in ida02.
overviews.ida02:
If chapter ida01 establishes what an image is, chapter ida02 establishes the fundamental operations that transform images. Three mathematical frameworks are developed in parallel — spatial filtering via convolution, structural analysis via morphology, and frequency-domain analysis via the Fourier transform — and understanding their interrelationships is central to everything that follows in the course.

Discrete convolution is defined as $(I * h)[x,y] = \sum_{u,v} I[x-u, y-v]\, h[u,v]$, where $h$ is the filter kernel. The sign flip in the indices (correlation uses $+u,+v$; convolution uses $-u,-v$) matters when the kernel is not symmetric, but is irrelevant for symmetric kernels like Gaussians. The Gaussian filter $G(x,y;\sigma) = \frac{1}{2\pi\sigma^2}\exp\!\left(-\frac{x^2+y^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$ is separable: $G(x,y;\sigma) = G(x;\sigma)\cdot G(y;\sigma)$. Separability enables a $M\times N$ convolution with a $k\times k$ kernel in $O(MNk)$ operations instead of $O(MNk^2)$ — a factor of $k$ reduction that matters enormously for large $k$. Gaussian smoothing at scale $\sigma$ removes structures finer than $\approx 2\sigma$ pixels, making it the canonical scale-space operator. The Laplacian of Gaussian (LoG) $\nabla^2 G$ combines smoothing with second-derivative sharpening: it detects blobs and edges as zero-crossings, and is equivalent to the difference of two Gaussians (DoG) at slightly different scales — a fact exploited by SIFT (chapter ida13).

Edge detection exemplifies the signal-processing perspective on images. The Sobel operator approximates the first spatial derivative: $\nabla I \approx (I * S_x, I * S_y)$ where $S_x$ and $S_y$ are $3\times3$ antisymmetric kernels. The gradient magnitude $|\nabla I| = \sqrt{(I*S_x)^2 + (I*S_y)^2}$ peaks at edges; the gradient direction $\theta = \arctan\!\left(\frac{I*S_y}{I*S_x}\right)$ indicates the edge orientation. The Canny detector (1986) adds two refinements: non-maximum suppression thins wide ridges to single-pixel edges by keeping only local maxima in the gradient direction; hysteresis thresholding uses two thresholds $T_{\text{low}}$ and $T_{\text{high}}$ to accept edge pixels — strong above $T_{\text{high}}$, weak only if connected to a strong pixel. This produces thin, connected edges robust to noise, and remains one of the most widely used preprocessing steps despite being over 35 years old.

The morphological operations introduced in ida01 receive their rigorous algebraic treatment here. Erosion and dilation are dual under complement: $\overline{A \oplus B} = \bar{A} \ominus B$, meaning dilation of the foreground equals erosion of the background. The hit-or-miss transform detects specific shapes by simultaneously eroding with a foreground pattern and eroding the complement with a background pattern, returning only pixels where both match simultaneously. Morphological gradient $A \oplus B - A \ominus B$ outlines object boundaries. These operations are exact and interpretable — important in scientific imaging contexts where a pixel-level explanation of every decision is required, as seen in the biological segmentation methods of chapter ida05.

The Discrete Fourier Transform (DFT) decomposes an image into complex sinusoids: $F(u,v) = \sum_{x=0}^{M-1}\sum_{y=0}^{N-1} I(x,y)\exp\!\left(-2\pi i\left(\frac{ux}{M}+\frac{vy}{N}\right)\right)$. The central coefficient $F(0,0)$ is the mean intensity (the DC component); coefficients at large $(u,v)$ capture fine spatial detail (high frequencies). The convolution theorem is the DFT's most powerful result: $\mathcal{F}\{I*h\} = \mathcal{F}\{I\} \cdot \mathcal{F}\{h\}$. Convolution in the spatial domain becomes pointwise multiplication in the frequency domain. For large kernels, it is faster to compute the DFT of both, multiply, and inverse-transform than to convolve directly. Frequency-domain analysis also makes the physics of filtering transparent: a low-pass filter (retain small $(u,v)$) blurs the image; a high-pass filter (retain large $(u,v)$) reveals edges; a notch filter removes specific spatial frequencies such as scan-line artefacts in microscopy. Truncating the frequency spectrum before inverse transform introduces Gibbs phenomenon (ringing near sharp edges) — the price paid for abrupt spectral cuts. The DFT perspective directly motivates why CNNs (ida03, ML ch09) learn to behave as adaptive band-pass filter banks.

Exercise bağlantısı — Exercise 02: Bu chapter'ın pratik uygulaması. 3D floresan mikroskobi görüntüsü (Arabidopsis thaliana hücre zarı) üzerinde önce gamma düzeltmesi ile kontrast artırımı, ardından median filtre ile gürültü temizleme (zarları koruyarak), Gaussian filtre ve separable filter denkliğinin doğrulanması. Frekans alanında FFT + ball şeklinde low-pass filtre uygulaması. Son olarak morphological erosion ile instance segmentation maskından hücre zarı sınırlarını çıkarma.
overviews.ida03:
Chapters ida01 and ida02 develop classical, hand-engineered image analysis tools. Chapter ida03 introduces the data-driven paradigm that has superseded most of them: deep learning. Rather than deriving a specific algorithm for each image task, a neural network is trained end-to-end to learn the appropriate transformation directly from data. The chapter serves as a self-contained crash course, covering the same material as ML chapters ch07 (neural networks), ch08 (optimisation), and ch09 (CNNs) but with an emphasis on the image-specific design choices that make deep models work.

The multilayer perceptron (MLP) begins from the perceptron: a single linear threshold unit $\hat{y} = \text{sign}(w^\top x + b)$. The XOR problem proved that no single hyperplane can classify all configurations, motivating hidden layers: with one hidden layer computing intermediate features, the input space is non-linearly remapped so that a final linear layer can separate any configuration. The formal MLP update rule is $a^{(l)} = g\!\left(W^{(l)} a^{(l-1)} + b^{(l)}\right)$, where $g$ is the elementwise activation function. Without $g$, the composition of linear layers collapses to a single linear transformation — depth becomes meaningless. The Universal Approximation Theorem guarantees that one hidden layer suffices to approximate any continuous function, but the exponential number of neurons required motivates the use of many layers with polynomial parameter counts. ReLU $\max(0,z)$ is the standard activation: its gradient is 1 for $z>0$ and 0 otherwise, preventing gradient attenuation for positive values and enabling training of very deep networks.

Backpropagation (Rumelhart et al., 1986) computes the gradient of the scalar loss with respect to every parameter in a single backward pass. Define error signals $\delta^{(l)} = \partial \mathcal{L}/\partial z^{(l)}$. At the output layer: $\delta^{(L)} = \frac{\partial\mathcal{L}}{\partial a^{(L)}} \odot g'(z^{(L)})$. For each hidden layer: $\delta^{(l)} = \left(W^{(l+1)}\right)^\top \delta^{(l+1)} \odot g'(z^{(l)})$. Weight gradients: $\partial\mathcal{L}/\partial W^{(l)} = \delta^{(l)}(a^{(l-1)})^\top$. The key property is that computing all gradients costs $O(1)$ backward passes regardless of the number of parameters — not one pass per parameter — making training feasible for models with millions of weights. Vanishing gradients in deep networks (products of weight matrices and activation derivatives shrinking exponentially) are mitigated by ReLU activations, batch normalisation, and residual connections.

Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) are the canonical architecture for image data, introduced in ML ch09 and applied throughout this course. Local receptive fields (each unit sees only a small patch), weight sharing (the same filter slides over the entire image), and pooling (local aggregation for translation invariance) together reduce parameters by orders of magnitude compared to fully-connected layers while encoding spatial inductive biases. A convolutional layer with $C_{\text{out}}$ filters of size $k\times k$ on input with $C_{\text{in}}$ channels has $k^2 C_{\text{in}} C_{\text{out}}$ parameters — independent of image size. Stacking convolutional layers, non-linearities, and pooling builds a hierarchy: early layers detect edges and textures; deep layers respond to semantic categories. This hierarchical feature learning is precisely what the subsequent detection and segmentation architectures (ida04, ida05) exploit.

The loss function and output activation must be jointly designed for the task. For regression (e.g., bounding box coordinates), a linear output with MSE loss $\mathcal{L} = \|y - \hat{y}\|^2$ is appropriate. For binary classification (foreground/background in segmentation), a sigmoid output $\hat{p} = \sigma(z)$ with binary cross-entropy $\mathcal{L} = -[t\log\hat{p} + (1-t)\log(1-\hat{p})]$ is standard. For $K$-class classification, softmax outputs $\hat{p}_k = \exp(z_k)/\sum_j\exp(z_j)$ with categorical cross-entropy. A critical design for imbalanced datasets — prevalent in detection where most locations are background — is the focal loss $\text{FL}(p_t) = -(1-p_t)^\gamma \log(p_t)$, which down-weights well-classified easy examples by factor $(1-p_t)^\gamma$ (typically $\gamma=2$), forcing the model to concentrate on the rare hard positives. Focal loss is the enabling component of RetinaNet and dense object detectors discussed in chapter ida04.

Exercise bağlantısı — Exercise 03: Bu chapter'ın uygulamalı karşılığı. Perceptron ile AND/OR mantık kapılarını sıfırdan implement etme ve lineer ayrılabilirlik sınırını görme. MLP ile sinüs fonksiyonu tahmini (Keras, farklı layer derinlikleri karşılaştırması). LeNet benzeri CNN ile MNIST rakam sınıflandırması — %98.6 test accuracy, öğrenilen kernel'ların görselleştirilmesi ve katmanlar arası feature map analizi.
overviews.ida04:
Object detection asks not just 'what is in this image?' but 'where, and how many?'. This extension from classification to localisation constitutes one of the most impactful problems in computer vision, with direct applications in autonomous driving, medical image analysis, surveillance, and robotics. The chapter traces the evolution from classical sliding-window approaches to modern single-pass deep detectors, each transition motivated by a specific bottleneck in speed or accuracy.

Classical detection relied on template matching — scanning a reference patch over the image and finding locations of high correlation. This is conceptually equivalent to a single convolution (ida02 convolution theorem) but fails for objects with intra-class variability (different instances of 'car' look very different). Sliding-window detectors overcome this by training a strong binary classifier (an SVM with HOG features, or the Viola-Jones cascade for faces) and applying it exhaustively at multiple scales and aspect ratios. The Viola-Jones detector introduced two critical ideas: integral images for $O(1)$ feature computation at any scale, and cascaded classifiers that reject most windows at the first stage and only devote computation to promising candidates. Despite impressive real-time face detection performance, the hand-engineered feature pipeline fundamentally limits generalisation.

R-CNN (Girshick et al., 2014) replaced hand-crafted features with deep CNN representations. The pipeline is: (1) selective search generates $\approx2000$ region proposals per image based on colour and texture similarity; (2) each proposal is warped to $227\times227$ and forward-passed through a CNN independently; (3) the resulting features feed an SVM classifier and a bounding-box regressor. The accuracy was transformative; the speed was not — 47 seconds per image on a GPU, because $2000$ independent CNN passes are required. Fast R-CNN (2015) fixed the efficiency bottleneck by inverting the order of operations: run the CNN once on the whole image to get a shared feature map, then extract per-region features with RoI Pooling — a spatial max-pooling operation that maps any-sized region to a fixed $7\times7$ grid by dividing the region into a $7\times7$ grid and max-pooling within each cell. This reduced inference to $\approx2$ seconds per image, with the bottleneck now in selective search proposal generation.

Faster R-CNN (2015) eliminated selective search entirely by introducing the Region Proposal Network (RPN). The RPN slides a small network over the convolutional feature map and, at each spatial location, predicts objectness scores and bounding-box offsets for $k$ anchor boxes — predefined boxes of different scales and aspect ratios centred at that location. Anchors with IoU $>0.7$ with a ground-truth box are positive; IoU $<0.3$ are negative. The RPN and the detection head share the convolutional backbone, making the entire system trainable end-to-end. Inference requires $\approx0.2$ seconds per image — real-time for many applications. Faster R-CNN remains a standard two-stage baseline: high accuracy at the cost of two sequential networks.

YOLO (You Only Look Once, Redmon et al., 2016) reformulated detection as a single regression problem. The image is divided into an $S\times S$ grid (e.g., $7\times7$); each cell predicts $B$ bounding boxes parameterised as $(x,y,w,h,\text{confidence})$ and $C$ class probability vectors. Confidence is $P(\text{object}) \times \text{IoU}_{\text{pred,truth}}$. All predictions are made in a single forward pass, enabling 45 FPS detection on a GPU. The price is reduced accuracy on small objects and objects whose centres fall in the same cell. Feature Pyramid Networks (FPN, Lin et al., 2017) address multi-scale detection by adding a top-down pathway with lateral connections to the standard bottom-up CNN backbone. Each resolution level in the pyramid receives both fine-grained spatial information from the bottom-up path and rich semantic information from the top-down path, enabling accurate detection of objects across a wide range of scales. FPN is now a standard component in virtually all modern detectors. The focal loss introduced in ida03 was specifically designed for RetinaNet, a single-stage detector with FPN that achieves two-stage accuracy at single-stage speed — resolving the long-standing speed-accuracy trade-off.

Exercise bağlantısı — Exercise 04: İki ayrı görev. İlk kısımda PyTorch'ta pretrained ResNet50-FPN backbone'lu Faster R-CNN ile inference — proposal kutuları ve final tespitlerin görselleştirilmesi. İkinci kısımda sıfırdan YOLO-tarzı tek aşamalı hücre dedektörü: Human U2OS hücreleri (BBBC006, public dataset), 32×32 grid tabanlı çıktı, score/offset/size için ayrı FC head'ler, ResNet50 backbone, 50 epoch eğitim.
overviews.ida05:
Detection (ida04) answers 'where is the object?' at bounding-box granularity. Segmentation goes further: it answers 'which pixel belongs to which object?' — a dense prediction problem requiring a label for every one of the $M\times N$ pixels in the image. This is both technically harder and scientifically more valuable: in biology, exact cell boundaries determine morphological measurements; in autonomous driving, knowing the precise road boundary is safety-critical.

Classical segmentation begins with global thresholding, the simplest possible region assignment. Otsu's method (1979) finds the threshold $T^*$ that minimises the weighted within-class variance of the two resulting regions, equivalent to maximising between-class variance. It derives $T^*$ analytically from the histogram — an $O(L)$ computation regardless of image size. Otsu assumes a bimodal intensity distribution; when this fails (uneven illumination, multiple objects), adaptive thresholding and more sophisticated methods are needed. The watershed algorithm treats the image gradient magnitude as a topographic surface: water poured into local minima rises and eventually meets water from adjacent basins, forming watershed lines — the segmentation boundaries. Watershed produces closed contours and handles complex topology but is sensitive to noise-induced local minima, requiring marker-controlled variants. Graph cuts formulate segmentation as a minimum-cut problem: pixels are graph nodes, edge weights encode colour similarity, and finding the minimum cut between source (foreground) and sink (background) terminals gives the globally optimal binary segmentation under the energy model — tractable via max-flow algorithms.

U-Net (Ronneberger et al., 2015) is the architecture that transformed biomedical image segmentation. The motivation was specific: biomedical datasets contain tens to hundreds of images rather than millions; the network must localise precisely (no image-level labels); and cell boundaries require fine-grained spatial resolution. U-Net's encoder-decoder architecture with skip connections addresses all three simultaneously. The encoder (contracting path) applies repeated $3\times3$ convolutions with $2\times2$ max-pooling, doubling channels at each level while halving spatial resolution — standard CNN feature extraction. The decoder (expansive path) upsamples via transposed convolutions, but critically, concatenates the corresponding encoder feature map via skip connections at each resolution level. These skip connections provide the decoder with precise spatial information that was lost during downsampling. The input is $572\times572$ but the output is $388\times388$ (valid convolutions, not padded) — the prediction covers only the central region to avoid boundary artefacts. Trained with aggressive data augmentation (elastic deformations, rotations, flips), U-Net achieves state-of-the-art results with as few as 30 training images.

Mask R-CNN (He et al., 2017) extends Faster R-CNN to instance segmentation: distinguishing between individual object instances, not just semantic categories. The extension adds a third head (alongside classification and bounding-box regression) that predicts a binary mask for each detected region. The critical technical contribution is RoI Align, which replaces the quantised RoI Pooling of Fast R-CNN with bilinear interpolation — avoiding the integer rounding errors that misalign features by up to 0.5 pixels. For $28\times28$ mask prediction, this misalignment is devastating; RoI Align eliminates it at negligible computational cost, substantially improving mask accuracy. Mask R-CNN achieves state-of-the-art instance segmentation and serves as the baseline for the biology-specific methods below.

Segmentation metrics are essential for model evaluation and comparison. Intersection over Union $\text{IoU} = |A\cap B|/|A\cup B|$ measures overlap between predicted and ground-truth regions; the Dice coefficient $\text{Dice} = 2|A\cap B|/(|A|+|B|)$ is mathematically equivalent to the F1-score and more sensitive near zero-overlap. Cellpose (Stringer et al., 2021) takes a fundamentally different approach for biological cell segmentation: instead of predicting masks directly, it predicts a gradient flow field pointing from every pixel toward the centre of its owning cell. Following these gradients by simulation groups pixels into cells — the method handles cells of arbitrary shape including elongated, branching, and densely packed morphologies that confound mask-based approaches. StarDist predicts for each pixel a set of radial distances to the cell boundary in $n$ directions, fitting a star-convex polygon — a shape that is the most natural representation for typical round/oval cell shapes in fluorescence microscopy. Both Cellpose and StarDist illustrate how domain knowledge of object geometry can be incorporated into neural network output parameterisations rather than treated as post-processing.

Exercise bağlantısı — Exercise 05: StarDist ve Cellpose ile pratik hücre segmentasyonu. StarDist kısmında 4–128 vertex arasında polygon temsil doğruluğu karşılaştırması (32'nin neden optimal olduğunun görülmesi), ardından pretrained 2D_demo modeliyle test görüntülerinde inference ve polygon görselleştirme. Cellpose kısmında pretrained cyto modeliyle vertikal/horizontal gradient ve semantic segmentation çıktılarının incelenmesi, gradient tracking ile final instance maskelerinin üretilmesi.

Lab bağlantısı — Lab Session 5 (CNN for Segmentation): Pascal VOC segmentation dataset üzerinde semantic segmentation. VGG'nin fully connected katmanlarını Conv'a dönüştürerek FCN yapma, transposed convolution ile spatial boyutu geri kazanma, skip connection ile farklı ölçeklerden feature birleştirme. mIoU ve Dice score ile değerlendirme. Bonus olarak tam U-Net encoder-decoder mimarisi. Bu lab'ı IDA exercise 05'teki instance segmentation (StarDist, Cellpose) ile birlikte düşününce semantic → instance segmentation farkı daha net oturuyor.
overviews.ida06:
Detection and segmentation (ida04, ida05) operate on individual frames. Many applications — autonomous driving, multi-object sports analysis, biological cell migration — require tracking objects continuously through time, associating detections across frames and maintaining persistent identities even through occlusions and re-entries. This chapter covers three complementary techniques: probabilistic state estimation with the Kalman filter, combinatorial assignment with the Hungarian algorithm, and dense motion estimation with optical flow.

The Kalman filter (Kalman, 1960) is the optimal linear estimator for a state-space model subject to Gaussian noise. The system model defines state dynamics: $\hat{x}_k = A\hat{x}_{k-1} + B u_k$ (prediction step), where $A$ is the transition matrix (e.g., constant-velocity motion: $A$ integrates velocity into position), $B u_k$ is optional control input, and prediction uncertainty grows as $P_k = A P_{k-1} A^\top + Q$ where $Q$ is the process noise covariance. The update step incorporates a new measurement $z_k$: compute the Kalman gain $K = P_k H^\top (H P_k H^\top + R)^{-1}$ where $H$ maps state to measurement space and $R$ is measurement noise covariance. The state and covariance are then corrected: $\hat{x}_k \leftarrow \hat{x}_k + K(z_k - H\hat{x}_k)$ and $P_k \leftarrow (I - KH)P_k$.
